steps.title: Block the gate frame (pencil)
steps.description: Make sure you're in pencil mode. Lightly sketch a tall rectangle for the overall gate shape — taller than wide, with a curved or arched top if you like. This is the outer frame.

steps.title: Find the angles (pencil)
steps.description: Notice the angle of the top edge — is it perfectly horizontal, or slightly tilted because the gate is set into a wall that recedes? Sketch the tilt as you actually see it. Same check for the sides.

steps.title: Add the vertical slats (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, mark four or five evenly spaced vertical lines inside the frame for the gate's slats. Then add two short horizontal bars — one near the top, one near the bottom — that the slats attach to.

steps.title: Refine and add the handle (pencil)
steps.description: Look across your slats — are they all roughly parallel? If any drifted, erase and redraw. Then sketch a small ring or lever shape near the middle of one side for the gate's handle.

steps.title: Switch to ink and trace the frame
steps.description: Switch to ink mode. Trace the outer gate frame in confident strokes from your shoulder, not your wrist — long straight runs come out cleaner when you move from the elbow. Land each corner where the lines actually meet.

steps.title: Ink the slats and crossbars (ink)
steps.description: Still in ink, trace each vertical slat in a single committed pass, then the two horizontal crossbars over them. Where the bars cross the slats, the bar passes in front — stop the slat lines at the bar's edges.

steps.title: Ink the handle and hinges (ink)
steps.description: Trace the handle as a small ring or curve, then add two small rectangles where the gate meets the wall — those are the hinges. A few marks is enough; don't render every screw.

steps.title: Clean up
steps.description: Switch to the eraser and remove any pencil guide lines that distract from the ink gate.
